report_name: Post-Shai-Hulud: npm Attacks Evolve with Wormable Malware and CI/CD Persistence

threat_actors.name: TeamPCP
threat_actors.description: Threat actor attributed with the April 2026 campaign that impersonated the Bitwarden CLI tool on npm to steal credentials and propagate malware.

attack_patterns.name: Trusted Relationship
attack_patterns.description: The attacks abused the trust developers have in the npm package manager and its ecosystem to trick them into installing malicious packages.

attack_patterns.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
attack_patterns.description: A lightweight bootstrapper script (setup.mjs) was used to download a larger, main payload (execution.js) from a remote server after initial execution.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials
attack_patterns.description: The malware harvested a wide range of credentials, including cloud provider keys, developer tokens, and secrets from CI/CD systems.

attack_patterns.name: Application Layer Protocol
attack_patterns.description: The malware used GitHub's public commit search API (a web protocol) as a covert command-and-control and data exfiltration channel.

attack_patterns.name: Masquerading
attack_patterns.description: The malware propagated by injecting malicious code into other npm projects a compromised developer had access to, continuing the cycle of supply chain compromise.

attack_patterns.name: Match Legitimate Name or Location
attack_patterns.description: A malicious package was published as `@bitwarden/cli`, impersonating the legitimate Bitwarden command-line tool to deceive developers.

attack_patterns.name: Supply Chain Compromise
attack_patterns.description: Adversaries compromised legitimate, high-traffic SAP packages in the npm registry to distribute malware to a large base of enterprise developers.

attack_patterns.name: Event Triggered Execution
attack_patterns.description: The attack was initiated by abusing npm's lifecycle scripts, specifically by adding a `preinstall` hook to the `package.json` file, which automatically executed a malicious script (`setup.mjs`) upon installation.

attack_patterns.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
attack_patterns.description: The main payload (`execution.js`) was heavily obfuscated using a custom method labeled `ctf-scramble-v2`, based on a seeded Fisher-Yates shuffle algorithm, to hinder analysis.

attack_patterns.name: OS Credential Dumping
attack_patterns.description: The malware used a Python helper to read the `/proc` memory of the `GitHub Actions Runner.Worker` process to extract masked secrets and credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Scheduled Task/Job
attack_patterns.description: Persistence was established within the victim's CI/CD pipeline by pushing a malicious GitHub Actions workflow file (`.github/workflows/auto-update.yml`) to their repositories.

attack_patterns.name: Data from Local System
attack_patterns.description: The malware payload aggressively searched for and stole sensitive data from local configuration files, including credentials for AWS, GCP, and Azure, as well as npm tokens and SSH keys.

attack_patterns.name: Exfiltration Over Web Service
attack_patterns.description: Stolen tokens were exfiltrated by creating commits on public GitHub repositories with the Base64-encoded tokens embedded in the commit messages.

malware.name: Shai-Hulud
malware.description: A pivotal, self-replicating worm from September 2025 that targeted the npm registry and served as a foundation for subsequent, more sophisticated supply chain attacks.

malware.name: Bitwarden CLI Impersonation Malware
malware.description: A multi-stage payload used in the TeamPCP campaign in April 2026. It impersonated the `@bitwarden/cli` npm package, stole a wide range of credentials, and exhibited worm-like behavior to propagate to other npm projects. Artifacts contained the string 'Shai-Hulud: The Third Coming'.

malware.name: Mini Shai-Hulud Payload
malware.description: A multi-stage, heavily obfuscated payload used in the 'Mini Shai-Hulud' campaign of April 2026. It was delivered via a `preinstall` hook in compromised SAP CAP packages to harvest developer secrets, cloud credentials, and CI/CD tokens from infected systems.

tools.name: npm
tools.description: The npm package manager and registry were the primary vector for this supply chain attack, with attackers publishing malicious packages and abusing lifecycle hooks.

tools.name: Node.js
tools.description: The Node.js runtime was used to execute the malicious `setup.mjs` bootstrapper and the main `execution.js` payload.

tools.name: Python
tools.description: A Python helper script was embedded in the main payload to read the /proc memory of the GitHub Actions Runner process to steal masked secrets.

tools.name: GitHub API
tools.description: The legitimate GitHub commit search API was abused by the malware as a covert command-and-control and data exfiltration channel.

campaigns.name: @bitwarden/cli Impersonation
campaigns.description: A supply chain attack campaign in April 2026, attributed to TeamPCP, that involved publishing a malicious npm package impersonating the Bitwarden CLI tool. The malware stole credentials and self-propagated to other projects.

campaigns.name: Mini Shai-Hulud
campaigns.description: A supply chain attack campaign in April 2026 that compromised four high-traffic SAP CAP and MTA build toolchain packages on npm. The campaign used a multi-stage payload to harvest developer credentials and established persistence in CI/CD pipelines.

identities.name: Bitwarden
identities.description: A legitimate password manager whose command-line tool (CLI) was impersonated by a malicious npm package in a supply chain attack.

identities.name: SAP
identities.description: A major enterprise software company whose popular Cloud Application Programming (CAP) and MTA build toolchain packages were compromised in the 'Mini Shai-Hulud' npm supply chain attack.

identities.name: GitHub
identities.description: A software development platform whose public commit search API was abused as a covert command-and-control channel by the malware. Its CI/CD system, GitHub Actions, was also targeted for persistence.

identities.name: npm
identities.description: The public JavaScript package registry that was the target of the supply chain attacks.

identities.name: AWS
identities.description: Amazon Web Services. The malware was designed to harvest credentials for this cloud provider from developer configuration files.

identities.name: GCP
identities.description: Google Cloud Platform. The malware was designed to harvest credentials for this cloud provider from developer configuration files.

identities.name: Azure
identities.description: Microsoft Azure. The malware was designed to harvest credentials for this cloud provider from developer configuration files.

infrastructure.name: GitHub Commit Search C2 Channel
infrastructure.description: The malware used GitHub's public commit search API as a dead-drop C2 mechanism. It would search for commits containing a specific keyword and exfiltrate stolen tokens by creating new commits with the data encoded in the commit message.

indicators.name: Malicious NPM Supply Chain Filenames
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Malicious NPM Supply Chain Filenames
id: 7c5a1b9e-8d0f-4e6a-9c3b-2a1f8e7d6c5b
status: experimental
description: Detects the presence of filenames associated with recent NPM supply chain attacks, including the Bitwarden impersonation and Mini Shai-Hulud campaigns.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-02
tags:
  - attack.t1195
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith:
      - '\setup.mjs'
      - '\execution.js'
      - '\bw1.js'
      - '\claude.js'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the creation or presence of files with names used in the npm supply chain attacks, such as the `setup.mjs` bootstrapper and `execution.js` payload.

indicators.name: Malicious NPM Preinstall Hook Execution
indicators.pattern:
title: Malicious NPM Preinstall Hook Execution
id: f8e7d6c5-b2a1-4f8e-8d0f-9c3b7a1b9e7c
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of a 'setup.mjs' script by Node.js, a TTP used in recent npm supply chain attacks to trigger a malicious payload via a 'preinstall' hook.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-02
tags:
  - attack.t1546
  - attack.t1059.007
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\node.exe'
    CommandLine|contains: 'setup.mjs'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the execution of `node setup.mjs`, which is the command triggered by the malicious `preinstall` hook in the compromised `package.json` files.

indicators.name: Covert C2 via GitHub Commit Search API
indicators.pattern:
title: Covert C2 via GitHub Commit Search API
id: a1b9e7c5-f8e7-4d6c-8d0f-5b2a1f8e7d6c
status: experimental
description: Detects network traffic to the GitHub commit search API containing keywords used as a covert C2 channel in recent npm supply chain attacks.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-02
tags:
  - attack.t1071.001
  - attack.t1567
level: high
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    cs-host: 'api.github.com'
    c-uri|contains|all:
      - '/search/commits'
      - '?q='
    c-uri|contains:
      - 'OhNoWhatsGoingOnWithGitHub'
      - 'LongLiveTheResistanceAgainstMachines'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects network requests to the GitHub commit search API that include specific keywords (`OhNoWhatsGoingOnWithGitHub`, `LongLiveTheResistanceAgainstMachines`) used by the malware for its C2 dead-drop mechanism.

relationships.source_name: Covert C2 via GitHub Commit Search API
